recipes.name: Sledging Biscuits

Main: 2 tbsp butter or lard (optional, for richness)

steps: Preheat oven to 150°C.
In a bowl, mix flour and salt.
Gradually add water while mixing until a stiff dough forms.
Knead the dough until smooth and dense (5 minutes).
Roll the dough out to about 1cm thickness.
Cut into 5x5cm squares or rectangles.
Prick each biscuit several times with a fork to prevent puffing.
Place on a baking sheet.
Bake for 45-50 minutes until hard, dry, and lightly golden.
Let cool completely.
Store in an airtight container—these will keep for months.
making_tips: Traditional sledging biscuits were unleavened and cooked twice to remove all moisture.
The biscuits should be rock-hard and very dry—this prevents spoilage.
Pricking with a fork is essential to keep them flat.
Explorers ate these with butter, cheese, or pemmican.
To serve, they were often crumbled into hoosh or softened with hot water.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: These are survival biscuits, designed for extreme conditions—commercial crackers are softer and less durable.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Whole wheat flour provides fiber and B vitamins. The biscuits are simple, enduring energy.

recipes.name: Pemmican (Traditional Energy Bar)

Main: 500g lean beef or bison, thinly sliced

steps: Dry the meat: Place sliced meat in a dehydrator or oven at 70°C for 8-10 hours until completely dry and brittle.
Grind the dried meat into a fine powder using a food processor.
Melt the rendered suet or tallow over low heat until liquid.
In a bowl, combine the ground meat powder, melted fat, and dried berries.
Mix thoroughly until it forms a paste.
Press the mixture into a greased pan or mold.
Chill until firm.
Cut into bars.
Wrap in parchment paper.
Store in a cool, dry place—pemmican lasts for years.
making_tips: The meat must be completely dried—any moisture will cause spoilage.
Traditional pemmican uses equal parts meat and fat by weight.
This was the ultimate survival food for polar explorers.
The fat provides energy, the meat provides protein.
Pemmican was often eaten frozen or crumbled into hoosh.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Commercial energy bars have sugar and preservatives. Pemmican is pure meat and fat—real survival food.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Meat provides complete protein and iron. Fat offers dense energy. Berries add vitamin C.

recipes.name: Hoosh (Explorer's Stew)

Main: 200g pemmican (or substitute with beef jerky powder + tallow)

steps: Crush the sledging biscuits into small pieces.
If using pemmican, chop it into small chunks.
Bring water to a boil in a pot.
Add the pemmican and simmer for 5 minutes.
Add the crushed biscuits.
Simmer for 10-15 minutes, stirring frequently.
The mixture will thicken into a porridge-like stew.
Season with salt.
Serve hot in bowls.
making_tips: Hoosh was the daily dinner of polar explorers.
The porridge should be thick and hearty.
Explorers often added extra fat for energy.
It was cooked on a small stove inside the tent.
Hoosh is simple, hot, and sustaining.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: This is survival food—simple, nutritious, and designed for extreme conditions.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Pemmican provides protein and fat. Biscuits add carbohydrates for energy.

recipes.name: Bannock (Explorer's Flatbread)
steps: Mix flour, baking powder, and salt in a bowl.
Cut in the fat until crumbly.
Add water gradually, mixing to a soft dough.
Knead gently on a floured surface.
Shape into a flat round, about 2cm thick.
Heat a heavy pan or griddle over a fire or stove.
Place the bannock in the pan.
Cook for 8-10 minutes per side until golden and cooked through.
Serve warm with butter, jam, or pemmican.
making_tips: Explorers cooked bannock in a frying pan over a Primus stove.
It could be wrapped around a stick and cooked over a fire.
Bannock doesn't require yeast, making it easy in the field.
The fat keeps it moist and adds energy.
This was a comforting fresh bread in Antarctica.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Fresh bannock was a luxury for explorers—commercial bread is softer and less durable.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Flour provides carbohydrates. Fat adds energy and vitamin A.

recipes.name: Frozen Fruit Cake (Explorer's Cake)

Cake: 300g mixed dried fruits (raisins, currants, candied peel)
Soaking: Extra brandy or rum for feeding

steps: Preheat oven to 150°C.
Cream butter and sugar until fluffy.
Add eggs one at a time, beating well.
Sift flour, baking powder, and spices together.
Fold dry ingredients into the butter mixture.
Fold in dried fruits and nuts.
Add brandy and mix well.
Pour into a lined cake tin.
Bake for 1.5-2 hours until a skewer comes out clean.
Cool completely.
Poke holes in the cake and feed with more brandy.
Wrap in alcohol-soaked cheesecloth and then in foil.
making_tips: The alcohol preserves the fruitcake for years.
Fruitcake was a staple of polar expeditions.
It was sometimes served frozen.
Explorers celebrated special occasions with fruitcake.
The cake improves with age.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Explorers' fruitcake was a luxury item—homemade is far superior.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Dried fruits provide iron and fiber. Nuts offer healthy fats.

recipes.name: Scotch Whisky (Antarctic Tradition)
steps: Pour whisky into a glass.
Add a splash of water if desired.
Serve neat or with a small amount of water.
Sip slowly to warm the body.
making_tips: Scotch was carried on many polar expeditions for morale and warmth.
Ernest Shackleton's whisky was famously recovered from the ice.
The whisky should be high quality—the best was taken on expeditions.
A small amount of water 'opens' the whisky's flavors.
The tradition lives on in modern Antarctic stations.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: An authentic Antarctic dram is best enjoyed in good company.

recipes.name: Glühwein (Mulled Wine)
steps: In a pot, combine grape juice, orange juice, and all spices.
Add orange peel and sugar.
Heat gently over low heat—do not boil.
Simmer for 15-20 minutes to infuse flavors.
Strain into mugs.
Serve warm.
Garnish with a cinnamon stick or orange slice.
making_tips: Midwinter Day (June 21) is celebrated in Antarctic stations with Glühwein.
The drink should be served warm, not hot.
The spices are traditional European mulled wine flavors.
Non-alcoholic versions are often served at German and French stations.
It warms the body and lifts morale during the dark winter.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Bottled mulled wine is less aromatic. Homemade allows control over spices.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Grape juice provides antioxidants. Cinnamon aids blood sugar regulation.
